perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE shall from time to time, upon request of FRANKLIN execute all papers and do all other things that may be reasonably required in order to protect the rights of FRANKLIN, and to vest in FRANKLIN or its successors or assigns the entire rights, title and interest in and to any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, program improvements, and applications for letters patent or copyright or maskwork registrations relating to anything pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN as provided above.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE shall from time to time, upon request of FRANKLIN execute all papers and do all other things that may be reasonably required in order to protect the rights of FRANKLIN relating to anything pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN as provided above.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'and to vest in FRANKLIN or its successors or assigns the entire rights, title and interest in and to any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, program improvements, and applications for letters patent or copyright or maskwork registrations' creates an in-text contradiction with Clause 1. Clause 1 states that the inventions become the property of Franklin. By removing this phrase, the employee is no longer explicitly obligated to ensure Franklin's ownership of these inventions is legally secured. This casts doubt on Franklin's ability to enforce ownership and protect its intellectual property rights as described in Clause 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: EMPLOYEE shall promptly and fully disclose to FRANKLIN any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements made by him or her pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN during his or her period of employment by FRANKLIN, and any improvements to his or her invention, writings, programs, and discoveries, made, conceived or acquired by him or her whether made or conceived solely or jointly with others, whether during regular business hours or otherwise; said Inventions, discoveries, writings, programs or improvements shall become and remain the property of FRANKLIN whether or not patent applications or copyright, trademark, or maskwork registrations are filed thereon.
perturbation.justification: The original text obligated the employee to execute papers to vest rights and title of inventions in FRANKLIN. The changed text removes this obligation. Clause 1 (Contradicted Text) states that the inventions become the property of Franklin. By removing the explicit obligation to vest ownership in Franklin, the changed text creates a direct contradiction because FRANKLIN's ownership is no longer legally secured by the employee's explicit action. This creates an in-text contradiction as per the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE further agrees not to divulge to any third party, either during his or her employment or thereafter, any confidential information conceived by him or her, disclosed by FRANKLIN, or obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to any of FRANKLIN'S processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, products, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby, and agrees to maintain this information in confidence until such time that such information has become widely known to the public or described in an issued patent or in a printed publication of wide circulation.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE further agrees not to divulge to any third party, either during his or her employment or thereafter, any confidential information conceived by him or her, disclosed by FRANKLIN, or obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to any of FRANKLIN'S processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, products, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and agrees to maintain this information in confidence until such time that such information has become widely known to the public or described in an issued patent or in a printed publication of wide circulation.', the clause removes the condition under which confidentiality obligations cease. This creates an in-text contradiction because the original clause establishes a clear endpoint to the confidentiality obligation, while removing it implies a potentially perpetual obligation, which might be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, conflicting with the intended duration outlined in the broader context.
perturbation.contradicted_text: EMPLOYEE further agrees not to divulge to any third party, either during his or her employment or thereafter, any confidential information conceived by him or her, disclosed by FRANKLIN, or obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to any of FRANKLIN'S processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, products, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby.
perturbation.justification: The original text includes a condition under which the confidentiality agreement ceases: when the information becomes widely known or published. The changed text removes this condition, implying a potentially perpetual confidentiality obligation. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text appears to assume a limited duration for confidentiality, as initially defined in the original (now removed) condition. Therefore, the removal of the clause creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of the employment, EMPLOYEE agrees to turn over to FRANKLIN all notes, memoranda, notebooks, drawings, records, customer lists, telephone files (including Rolodex and business card files) and correspondence in connection with anything done by him or her relating to his or her employment, for the reason that all confidential information contained therein is at all times the sole property of FRANKLIN.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of the employment, EMPLOYEE agrees to turn over to FRANKLIN all notes, memoranda, notebooks, drawings, records, customer lists, telephone files (including Rolodex and business card files) and correspondence in connection with anything done by him or her relating to his or her employment.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'for the reason that all confidential information contained therein is at all times the sole property of FRANKLIN' creates an in-text contradiction with Clause 1, which states that all inventions and improvements become the property of FRANKLIN. By removing the explicit declaration of ownership in this section, the enforceability of the obligation to return these materials becomes questionable. It weakens Franklin's claim to the materials, potentially allowing an employee to argue they are not obligated to return them because ownership is not clearly stated in this specific clause, creating conflict with the stated ownership intention elsewhere.
perturbation.contradicted_text: EMPLOYEE shall promptly and fully disclose to FRANKLIN any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements made by him or her pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN during his or her period of employment by FRANKLIN, and any improvements to his or her invention, writings, programs, and discoveries, made, conceived or acquired by him or her whether made or conceived solely or jointly with others, whether during regular business hours or otherwise; said Inventions, discoveries, writings, programs or improvements shall become and remain the property of FRANKLIN whether or not patent applications or copyright, trademark, or maskwork registrations are filed thereon.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that all confidential information contained in the listed materials is the sole property of FRANKLIN. The changed text removes this explicit statement of ownership. Clause 1, referenced as the 'contradicted text', states that all inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements become and remain the property of FRANKLIN. By removing the explicit declaration of ownership in the changed text, a contradiction arises. The employee could argue that the materials are not FRANKLIN's property because this specific clause doesn't explicitly state that. This contradicts the intention of Clause 1, making the obligation to return the materials questionable and creating uncertainty about ownership, leading to a clear and direct conflict within the document.
